content:
# Disagreements Log — Genesis Chapter 7 (GEN_007)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA (Σοφία — Wisdom)
**Agents:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Date of arbitration:** 2026-03-04
**Verses with recorded disagreements:** 7:1, 7:2, 7:3, 7:4, 7:6, 7:11, 7:12, 7:13, 7:16, 7:17–18, 7:19, 7:21–23, 7:22

---

## Disagreement Index

| # | Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | Consensus | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 7:1 | בֹּא — "Come" vs. "Go" | "Come" (implied) | "Come" | "Go" | "Come" | Minor |
| 2 | 7:2 | שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה — distributive rendering | "seven and seven" | "seven pairs" | "seven pairs" | "seven pairs" | Significant |
| 3 | 7:2 | אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ — "his female" vs. "its female" | "male and his female" | "a male and his female" | "a male and its female" | "a male and his female" | Minor |
| 4 | 7:3 | שָּׁמַיִם — "heavens" vs. "sky" | "heavens" | "heavens" | "sky" | "heavens" | Minor |
| 5 | 7:4 | מָחָה — "wipe away" / "wipe out" / "blot out" | "wipe away" | "blot out" | "wipe out" | "wipe out" | Minor |
| 6 | 7:4 | יְקוּם — "living thing" vs. "standing-thing" | "standing-thing" (literal) | "living thing" | "living thing" | "living thing" | Minor |
| 7 | 7:6 | Flood phrase — "the flood of waters" vs. "floodwaters" | "the flood was waters" | "the flood of waters" | "the floodwaters" | "the flood of waters" | Minor |
| 8 | 7:11 | Formatting of cosmological clauses | Prose sentence | Prose sentence | Verse lines after date formula | Modified verse formatting | Significant |
| 9 | 7:11 | מַעְיְנֹת — "fountains" vs. "springs" | "fountains" | "fountains" | "springs" | "fountains" | Minor |
| 10 | 7:11 | אֲרֻבֹּת — "windows" vs. "floodgates" | "windows" | "windows" | "floodgates" | "windows" | Significant |
| 11 | 7:11 | בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה — "on that very day" vs. "on that very same day" | "on this day" | "on that very day" | "on that very same day" | "on that very day" (7:11); "on that very same day" (7:13) | Minor |
| 12 | 7:12 | "Was upon the earth" vs. "fell on the earth" | "was upon the earth" | "was upon the earth" | "fell on the earth" | "was upon the earth" | Minor |
| 13 | 7:13 | Japheth transliteration | "Yaphet" | "Japheth" | "Japheth" | "Japheth" | Minor |
| 14 | 7:14 | "Every living thing" (חַיָּה) vs. "every beast" | "every living thing" | "every beast" | compressed | "every beast" | Minor |
| 15 | 7:16 | וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה בַּעֲדוֹ — "shut behind him" vs. "shut him in" | "shut behind him" | "shut him in" | "shut the door behind him" | "shut him in" | Significant |
| 16 | 7:16 | Formatting of v. 16b — inline vs. standalone | In-verse | Appended clause | Standalone paragraph | Standalone sentence with preceding break | Significant |
| 17 | 7:17 | נָשָׂא — "bore up" vs. "lifted" | "lifted" | "bore up" | "lifted" | "bore up" | Minor |
| 18 | 7:18 | גָּבַר first use — "prevailed" | "prevailed" | "prevailed" | "swelled" | "prevailed" | Significant |
| 19 | 7:19 | מְאֹד מְאֹד — double intensifier | "more and more" | "exceedingly greatly" | "more and more, greatly" | "more and more greatly" | Minor |
| 20 | 7:21 | גָּוַע — "expired" vs. "perished" | "expired" | "expired" | "perished" | "expired" | Minor |
| 21 | 7:21–23 | Formatting of death catalog | Prose | Prose | Broken verse | Modified verse (lines within verses) | Major |
| 22 | 7:22 | נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים — full compound vs. simplified | "breath of the spirit of life" | "breath of the spirit of life" | "the breath of life" (simplified) | "the breath of the spirit of life" | Major |
| 23 | 7:23 | Subject of וַיִּמַח — explicit God or ambiguous | "he wiped away" (ambiguous) | "every living thing was blotted out" (passive) | "He wiped out" (divine) | "He wiped out" | Minor |

---

## Detailed Disagreement Records

---

### Disagreement #1 — 7:1: בֹּא (bo') — "Come" vs. "Go"

**Hebrew:** בֹּא אַתָּה וְכָל-בֵּיתְךָ אֶל-הַתֵּבָה — "bo attah v'khol beitekha el ha-tevah"

**The question:** The imperative בֹּא (bo') is the second-person singular of בּוֹא ("to come/enter"). Should this be rendered "Come into the ark" or "Go into the ark"?

**EMET's position:** Renders with bracket notation but the underlying sense is "come" — EMET's extensive textual note on v. 1 observes the significance of לְפָנַי ("before me") without explicitly arbitrating "come" vs. "go" in the main text. The note implies "come."

**LOGOS's position:** "Come into the ark" — LOGOS notes explicitly (Translation Note #11): "The imperative בֹּא (bo, 'come') is the imperative of entry from within — not 'go into the ark' ... but 'come into the ark.' The use of בֹּא rather than לֵךְ implies that the LORD speaks from within the ark — inviting Noah to join him in the place of safety."

**PNEUMA's position:** "Go — you and all your household — into the ark." PNEUMA uses "Go" as the natural English entrance imperative and does not note the theological implication of בֹּא specifically. PNEUMA's rendering is communicatively natural; "Go into the ark" is what a modern English speaker would say.

**Consensus ruling:** "Come." The grammatical observation is correct: בֹּא is the imperative of בּוֹא, a verb of motion toward the speaker's location. The standard Hebrew entrance imperative for motion away from speaker is לֵךְ (lekh). The use of בֹּא preserves the possibility that the LORD speaks from a position of already being "in" the space of salvation — an ancient homiletical reading, first noted in the rabbinic tradition, and correct grammatically. "Go" is a natural English entrance imperative but is the wrong Hebrew word's translation; it imports לֵךְ where בֹּא stands. "Come" is the precise English equivalent and should be preserved.

**Note on minority position:** PNEUMA's "Go" is a reasonable dynamic rendering that prioritizes natural English over the Greek-and-Hebrew grammatical distinction. Readers of modern English translations (NIV: "Go into the ark"; NLT: "Go into the boat") should be aware that the Hebrew word is "come," not "go," and that the distinction is theologically significant in the tradition.

---

### Disagreement #2 — 7:2: שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה (shivah shivah) — distributive rendering

**Hebrew:** מִכֹּל הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּהוֹרָה תִּקַּח-לְךָ שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה — "from every clean animal take for yourself seven seven"

**The question:** How should the Hebrew distributive reduplication of the numeral seven be rendered in English?

**EMET's position:** "Seven and seven" — a literal representation of the repeated numeral, following the surface form of the Hebrew. EMET's note explains: "שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה is a distributive construction: 'seven seven' = 'seven each' = seven pairs (14 animals). This is confirmed by the parallel with vv. 8-9."

**LOGOS's position:** "Seven pairs" — an interpretive rendering that resolves the distributive into its English equivalent. LOGOS notes: "The formal translation renders this 'seven pairs' (following ESV, NASB), which captures the distributive sense. However, the Hebrew is literally 'seven seven' — not 'seven pairs' explicitly." LOGOS chooses "seven pairs" as "the most coherent English rendering of the distributive reduplication."

**PNEUMA's position:** "Seven pairs" — same as LOGOS; PNEUMA also uses "one pair" for the unclean animals, adding the word "one" (שְׁנַיִם, "two" = one pair) for clarity.

**Consensus ruling:** "Seven pairs." The distributive reduplication in Hebrew (NN + NN = "N each / N of each kind") is a recognized grammatical form with a clear English equivalent in the context. The fact that the immediately following phrase specifies "a male and his female" confirms that the distribution is into male-female pairs. The total is fourteen animals per clean species (seven males, seven females), and "seven pairs" accurately conveys this without requiring the reader to calculate. EMET's "seven and seven" is the more formally transparent rendering but risks being read as simple addition ("seven plus seven equals fourteen") rather than as a distributive pairing.

**The tension with Genesis 6:19–20:** The Priestly source commanded "two of every kind" (6:19–20); the Yahwist source here commands "seven pairs" of clean animals and "two" (one pair) of unclean. This is one of the clearest indicators of the Documentary Hypothesis's source analysis. The consensus translation preserves both commands without harmonization: 6:19–20 reads "two of every kind" (as rendered in GEN_006), and 7:2–3 reads "seven pairs" and "two" for the clean and unclean respectively. The difference is a feature of the received canonical text, not an error.

---

### Disagreement #3 — 7:2: אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ — "his female" vs. "its female"

**Hebrew:** אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ — literally "a man and his woman/wife"

**The question:** Should the Hebrew intimate-pairing vocabulary (אִישׁ = man, אִשְׁתּוֹ = his woman/wife) be rendered with the masculine possessive "his female" (preserving the relational Hebrew) or the neuter "its female" (normalizing to standard English animal reference)?

**EMET's position:** "Male and his female" — EMET notes explicitly: "the use of human relationship vocabulary (אִישׁ / אִשְׁתּוֹ) for animals is notable — it humanizes the pairing, or more precisely uses the standard Hebrew idiom for matched pairs in an intimate relationship."

**LOGOS's position:** "A male and his female" — same as EMET, with the indefinite article added.

**PNEUMA's position:** "A male and its female" — uses the neuter possessive, which is more conventional English for animals.

**Consensus ruling:** "A male and his female" (LOGOS). The Hebrew uses אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ — specifically the human-relationship word-pair, not the biological terms זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה ("male and female") that appear at v. 3 and v. 9 for the entry report. The distinction between the command language (intimate אִישׁ/אִשְׁתּוֹ) and the fulfillment language (categorical זָכָר/נְקֵבָה) is deliberate in the Hebrew. Rendering "its female" normalizes this away. The LXX (Vaticanus) already makes this leveling: ἄρσεν καὶ θῆλυ ("male and female") for the command, same for the entry — the intimate vocabulary is erased. The consensus preserves the MT's distinction.

---

### Disagreement #4 — 7:3: שָּׁמַיִם — "heavens" vs. "sky"

**Hebrew:** מֵעוֹף הַשָּׁמַיִם — "from the flying-things/birds of the heavens"

**EMET's position:** "Flying-things of the heavens"
**LOGOS's position:** "Birds of the heavens"
**PNEUMA's position:** "Birds of the sky"

**Consensus ruling:** "Birds of the heavens." Consistent with GEN_006 Disagreement #9 ruling. The Hebrew שָּׁמַיִם is the cosmological term throughout Genesis 1 (not merely the physical sky); the flood is a de-creation event and the cosmological vocabulary should be preserved. "Sky" (PNEUMA) is accurate but cosmologically flat.

On "birds" vs. "flying-things": EMET's "flying-things" is the more literal rendering of עוֹף, which encompasses all winged creatures. However, for v. 3 — where the category is being commanded for preservation — "birds of the heavens" is the more natural English collocation and is established in the received tradition. EMET's "flying-things" is preserved in the notes and in contexts where the taxonomic precision matters most (e.g., v. 14's full creature parade).

---

### Disagreement #5 — 7:4: מָחָה — "wipe away" / "wipe out" / "blot out"

**Hebrew:** וּמָחִיתִי אֶת-כָּל-הַיְקוּם אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתִי מֵעַל פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה

**EMET's position:** "Wipe away" (וּמָחִיתִי — future)
**LOGOS's position:** "Blot out"
**PNEUMA's position:** "Wipe out"

**Consensus ruling:** "Wipe out" — consistent with the GEN_006 ruling (Disagreement #8) on the same verb מָחָה. The consistency across chapters is essential: 6:7 ("I will wipe out"), 7:4 ("I will wipe out"), and 7:23 ("he wiped out") should all use the same English root to preserve the announced-and-fulfilled verbal arc. "Wipe away" (EMET) risks gentleness; "blot out" (LOGOS) is traditional and slightly more abstract; "wipe out" is violent, total, and consistent with the GEN_006 ruling.

---

### Disagreement #6 — 7:4, 7:23: יְקוּם (yequm) — "living thing" vs. "standing-thing"

**Hebrew:** כָּל-הַיְקוּם — from קוּם ("to stand/rise"), literally "that which stands"

**EMET's position:** "Every standing-thing" (7:4) — EMET provides an extensive note on this rare word, arguing for the literal rendering "standing-thing" to preserve the etymology (from קוּם, "to stand") and the word's rare, specific character.

**LOGOS's position:** "Every living thing" — the most common formal equivalence rendering; LOGOS notes that יְקוּם is "a relatively rare term for living beings" and renders it "living thing."

**PNEUMA's position:** "Every living thing" (implied in the dynamic rendering "every living thing I have made").

**Consensus ruling:** "Every living thing." EMET's "standing-thing" is etymologically precise and is confirmed by the LXX's ἀνάστημα ("standing thing / that which stands upright"). However, "standing-thing" is not established English usage, and the word's evident meaning in context — the collective of all upright living creatures — is best conveyed by "living thing." The etymology (from קוּם) is documented in the notes. The consensus does not adopt a nonce English coinage to render a rare Hebrew word when a natural English equivalent accurately conveys the referent.

---

### Disagreement #7 — 7:6: "The flood of waters" vs. "floodwaters"

**Hebrew:** וְהַמַּבּוּל הָיָה מַיִם עַל-הָאָרֶץ — "and the mabbul was waters upon the earth"

**EMET's position:** "The flood was waters upon the earth" — a very literal rendering that awkwardly presents the flood as "waters" (as if clarifying what kind of flood it was).

**LOGOS's position:** "The flood of waters came upon the earth" — following the formula, with the word מַיִם made into a genitive.

**PNEUMA's position:** "The floodwaters came upon the earth" — compounds the Hebrew two-word construction into a single English compound word.

**Consensus ruling:** "The flood of waters came upon the earth" (LOGOS). The Hebrew מַבּוּל (mabbul) is the technical term for this specific event, and מַיִם ("waters") is explicitly added — it is not redundant but emphatic. "Flood of waters" preserves both terms while reading naturally. PNEUMA's "floodwaters" is accurate but merges what the Hebrew keeps distinct. EMET's literal construction is awkward in English.

---

### Disagreement #8 — 7:11: Formatting of the cosmological clauses

**Hebrew:** בַּיּוֹם הַזֶּה נִבְקְעוּ כָּל-מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם רַבָּה וַאֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם נִפְתָּחוּ

**The question:** Should the two cosmological clauses of verse 11 be presented as prose (within the narrative flow) or as elevated verse lines (marking their structural significance)?

**EMET's position:** Full prose sentence, with the date formula and cosmological clauses all in one flowing sentence. The Hebrew parallels, while present, are embedded in what reads in the MT as a single declarative sentence.

**LOGOS's position:** Prose sentence, with a dash after "on that very day" to mark the transition, then the two clauses in sequence. LOGOS preserves the prose register while acknowledging the elevated content.

**PNEUMA's position:** Breaks after the date formula, then presents the two clauses as verse lines:

> *on that very day —*
> *all the springs of the great deep burst open,*
> *and the floodgates of the sky were thrown wide.*

PNEUMA's justification: "the cosmos comes apart" and the moment demands visual weight.

**Consensus ruling:** Modified verse formatting. The two clauses — "all the fountains of the great deep burst open" and "the windows of the heavens were opened" — are syntactically parallel (both Niphal passives) and thematically paired as the simultaneous reversal of Genesis 1:6–9. This is the kind of formally elevated diction — a parallelism marking a cosmological event — that the Hebrew poetic tradition uses as quasi-poetic heightening within narrative prose. While the Tiberian cantillation does not mark these verses as poetry (they use the standard prose accents visible in the Leningrad Codex), the narrative-liturgical function of the verse justifies visual differentiation.

The consensus breaks after the date formula and sets the two cosmological clauses on separate lines, preserving the verse boundary (7:11) but giving the moment its visual weight. This is neither full EMET/LOGOS prose nor full PNEUMA verse, but a hybrid that serves both the reference-text function and the literary weight of the passage.

**Note:** The Leningrad Codex manuscript (Folio 5r, GEN_007_page_002.jpg) presents verse 11 in the standard three-column prose format without special visual marking. The consensus formatting is therefore an interpretive choice of the arbiter, responsive to the text's literary-theological character rather than to any manuscript indication.

---

### Disagreement #9 — 7:11: מַעְיְנֹת — "fountains" vs. "springs"

**Hebrew:** מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם רַבָּה — "the [mayanot] of the great deep"

**EMET and LOGOS:** "Fountains of the great deep"
**PNEUMA:** "Springs of the great deep"

**Consensus ruling:** "Fountains of the great deep." Both "fountains" and "springs" are defensible translations of מַעְיְנֹת (from the root עַיִן, "eye / spring / source"). "Springs" is more natural in modern English for natural underground water sources. However, in the context of primordial cosmic water erupting through the floor of creation, "fountains" carries the grander, more violent connotation appropriate to the violence of נִבְקְעוּ ("burst open"). "Springs of the great deep" domesticates a cosmic eruption into a pastoral landscape feature. "Fountains of the great deep" has also been the established English rendering since the KJV (1611), and the consensus preserves this traditional phrasing for the most theologically loaded phrase in the chapter.

---

### Disagreement #10 — 7:11: אֲרֻבֹּת — "windows" vs. "floodgates"

**Hebrew:** אֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם נִפְתָּחוּ — "the [arubot] of the heavens were opened"

**The question:** The noun אֲרֻבָּה (arubba) means a latticed opening or window. Should it be rendered "windows" (preserving the architectural, cosmological image) or "floodgates" (capturing the hydraulic force of the water's release)?

**EMET's position:** "Windows of the heavens" — architectural term; EMET notes that the LXX's καταρράκται ("floodgates") is "a hydraulic image of controlled release," but EMET follows the MT's architectural "window" (arubba) reading.

**LOGOS's position:** "Windows of the heavens" — LOGOS notes at length that the formal translation follows MT's "windows" rather than LXX's "floodgates." The "windows" image connects explicitly to the Genesis 1 firmament structure: the firmament has windows that are normally latched; here they open.

**PNEUMA's position:** "Floodgates of the sky" — PNEUMA notes the LXX's καταρράκτης and judges "floodgates" more communicatively effective for modern readers who do not have the Genesis 1 cosmological architecture in mind. PNEUMA's argument: "the firmament's windows unlatched; the upper waters of Gen 1:7 return" — the reader needs to feel the release of force, and "floodgates" conveys that.

**Ancient witnesses:**
- LXX (Vaticanus): καταρράκται τοῦ οὐρανοῦ — "cataracts/floodgates of heaven"
- Targum Onkelos: kewwenin ("windows") — matches MT
- Peshitta: rubbe ("windows") — matches MT
- Vulgate: cataractae caeli — "cataracts/sluices of heaven" (matches LXX)

**Consensus ruling:** "Windows of the heavens." The MT's architectural image (the arubba as a latticed window or opening) is the more precise term, and it is the term that creates the deliberate connection to the Genesis 1:6–8 firmament structure. The reader who understands that the firmament in Genesis 1 is a solid expanse holding back the upper waters, and that this expanse has windows that are normally closed, immediately grasps the cosmological violence of "the windows were opened": the latches are thrown, the upper waters pour through. "Floodgates" communicates force but imports a hydraulic engineering image (a gate that controls water flow) that is not native to the Hebrew. The architectural image — windows — is native to the text and should be preserved.

**Minority position recorded:** PNEUMA's "floodgates" and the LXX tradition have significant support. Readers who find "windows of the heavens" puzzling without cosmological context should be directed to Genesis 1:6–8 (the creation of the firmament) for the background that makes the image intelligible.

---

### Disagreement #11 — 7:11 vs. 7:13: בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה — differentiated rendering

**The question:** The phrase בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה appears twice in the chapter — at v. 11 (in the date formula) and at v. 13 (introducing the family's entry). All three agents handle them identically. Should the consensus differentiate them?

**Rendering comparison:**
- EMET: "on this day" (v. 11) vs. "on this very day" (v. 13) — slight differentiation
- LOGOS: "on that very day" (v. 11) and "On that very day" (v. 13) — identical
- PNEUMA: "on that very day" (v. 11) and "On that very same day" (v. 13) — PNEUMA differentiates

**Consensus ruling:** The consensus differentiates: "on that very day" for v. 11, "On that very same day" for v. 13. The phrase is identical in Hebrew at both locations; however, the narrative function is different:
- In v. 11, it introduces the cosmic event (the fountains burst, the windows opened) and functions as a precision date marker.
- In v. 13, it links the human action (Noah's family entering) to the cosmic event already described (they entered on the same day the fountains burst and the sky opened).

The addition of "same" at v. 13 makes the cross-reference explicit: "on that very same day" implies "same as the day just described." This is the most natural English way to convey the narrative function that the repetition of the phrase creates.

---

### Disagreement #12 — 7:12: "Was upon the earth" vs. "fell on the earth"

**Hebrew:** וַיְהִי הַגֶּשֶׁם עַל-הָאָרֶץ — literally "and the rain was upon the earth"

**EMET and LOGOS:** "And the rain was upon the earth"
**PNEUMA:** "And the rain fell on the earth"

**Consensus ruling:** "And the rain was upon the earth." The Hebrew describes the rain's settled, sustained presence: "the rain was upon the earth" (וַיְהִי ... עַל-הָאָרֶץ). PNEUMA's "fell" captures the rain's descending motion (accurate but describing only the inception) rather than its persistent condition. The sustained presence of rain for forty days and forty nights is the point; "was upon the earth" conveys the weight of duration that "fell on the earth" does not.

---

### Disagreement #13 — 7:13: Japheth transliteration — "Yaphet" vs. "Japheth"

**Hebrew:** יֶפֶת (Yephet)

**EMET's position:** "Yaphet" — a more phonetically precise transliteration of the Hebrew יֶפֶת (the yod is rendered "Y", the pe remains "ph" / "f," the taw becomes "t"). EMET uses "Yaphet" throughout.

**LOGOS and PNEUMA's position:** "Japheth" — the established English transliteration, used since the KJV and maintained in virtually all English translations.

**Consensus ruling:** "Japheth." This is the established English rendering with four centuries of usage. "Yaphet" is more phonetically precise but creates unnecessary dissonance with every other English Bible, commentary, and liturgical tradition the reader is likely to encounter. The purpose of transliteration in a translation project is to provide English readers with a recognizable form; "Japheth" fulfills this purpose. EMET's phonetic precision is a transliteration standard, not a translation standard, and is better suited to a technical apparatus than a reading text. Consistent with GEN_006's ruling on the same name.

---

### Disagreement #14 — 7:14: חַיָּה — "every living thing" vs. "every beast"

**Hebrew:** כָּל-הַחַיָּה לְמִינָהּ — "every chayyah according to its kind"

**EMET's position:** "Every living thing according to its kind" — follows the generic rendering of חַיָּה as "living thing."

**LOGOS's position:** "Every beast according to its kind" — renders חַיָּה as "beast" (wild animal, as distinguished from בְּהֵמָה, domesticated livestock).

**PNEUMA's position:** Compresses the verse without specific treatment of the category distinctions.

**Consensus ruling:** "Every beast according to its kind" (LOGOS). In verse 14, all four creature categories are distinguished: חַיָּה (wild animals / beasts of the field), בְּהֵמָה (livestock / domesticated animals), רֶמֶשׂ (creeping things), and עוֹף (birds). LOGOS correctly distinguishes חַיָּה as wild animals ("beast") separate from the domesticated בְּהֵמָה ("livestock"). EMET's "living thing" for חַיָּה at this point is too generic and does not distinguish it from the broader category. The specificity of the creature-parade verse (v. 14) — precisely the verse that catalogs all the taxonomic kinds preserved in the ark — requires precise rendering.

---

### Disagreement #15 — 7:16: בַּעֲדוֹ — "shut behind him" vs. "shut him in"

**Hebrew:** וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה בַּעֲדוֹ

**The question:** The preposition בַּעֲד (ba'ad) carries two senses simultaneously: spatial-behind ("behind him") and protective-benefactive ("for him / on his behalf"). Which English preposition best captures both?

**EMET's position:** "And YHWH shut behind him" — preserving the spatial sense (the door is shut behind Noah, with Noah inside).

**LOGOS's position:** "And the LORD shut him in" — capturing the sealing-in sense; the suffix (-o, "him") is rendered as the object of the verb rather than the object of a preposition.

**PNEUMA's position:** "Then the LORD shut the door behind him" — adds "the door" (not in the Hebrew) and uses "behind him" (spatial).

**Consensus ruling:** "Then the LORD shut him in" (LOGOS). The prepositional phrase בַּעֲדוֹ functions here with the verb סָגַר (sagar, "to shut/close") to create the sense of total enclosure and protection: the LORD shut something around/behind/for Noah, sealing him inside. "Shut him in" captures both the physical completeness (he is now enclosed) and the protective agency (the LORD acted on Noah's behalf). "Shut behind him" (EMET, PNEUMA) is spatially accurate but lacks the protective weight of the benefactive sense of בַּעֲד.

The word "door" added by PNEUMA ("shut the door behind him") is not in the Hebrew. The MT says only "the LORD shut [behind him / him in]" — no explicit object. The door is implied but not named. Adding "the door" makes explicit what the Hebrew leaves in a more elliptical, intimate register. The consensus follows the MT's compression.

---

### Disagreement #16 — 7:16: Formatting — inline clause vs. standalone sentence

**The question:** Should "And the LORD shut him in" (v. 16b) be formatted as an appended clause within verse 16's running text, or set apart as a standalone sentence?

**EMET's position:** In-verse; verse 16 ends with the clause: "...as God had commanded him; and YHWH shut behind him." One continuous verse.

**LOGOS's position:** Two sentences within the verse, with a period after "as God had commanded him": "...as God had commanded him. And the LORD shut him in." The period creates a functional pause but the verse remains typographically unified.

**PNEUMA's position:** Standalone sentence on its own line, preceded by a paragraph break:

> *Those that came, male and female from all flesh, came just as God had commanded him.*
>
> *Then the LORD shut the door behind him.*

PNEUMA argues: "The structure of verse 16 up to this point has been bureaucratic... And then, in the last five words of the verse, the narrative halts and does something unprecedented." The visual separation marks this as a narrative pivot.

**Consensus ruling:** Standalone sentence with preceding line break. The consensus agrees with PNEUMA's structural insight: the divine-name change at this precise point — from Elohim (v. 16a) to YHWH (v. 16b) — marks a shift of register so significant that the sentence deserves its own visual space. The boarding of the ark (v. 16a) belongs to the ordered, taxonomic, cosmic Elohim narrative; the closing of the door (v. 16b) belongs to the intimate, covenantal, personal YHWH narrative. The paragraph break at this exact juncture is not an artificial imposition but a visual representation of the narrative's own theological structure.

LOGOS's solution (two sentences within the verse, separated by a period) is a reasonable middle position but is visually insufficient to mark the weight of the transition. The line break is the appropriate signaling device.

---

### Disagreement #17 — 7:17: נָשָׂא — "bore up" vs. "lifted"

**Hebrew:** וַיִּשְׂאוּ אֶת-הַתֵּבָה — "and they lifted/bore the ark"

**EMET and PNEUMA:** "Lifted the ark"
**LOGOS:** "Bore up the ark"

**Consensus ruling:** "Bore up the ark" (LOGOS). The verb נָשָׂא carries the sense of sustained carrying, bearing weight — not merely a momentary lift but the ongoing work of supporting and elevating. "Bore up" captures this weight-bearing quality that "lifted" (suggesting a single upward motion) does not. "Bore up" also has a slightly elevated register that suits the context (EMET notes that the related verb רוּם in the following clause means "to be exalted/raised" — vocabulary of divine elevation). "Bore up the ark" echoes an older English register fitting the solemnity of the scene.

---

### Disagreement #18 — 7:18: גָּבַר (gavar) first use — "prevailed" vs. "swelled"

**Hebrew:** וַיִּגְבְּרוּ הַמַּיִם וַיִּרְבּוּ מְאֹד עַל-הָאָרֶץ — "and the waters prevailed/strengthened and increased greatly upon the earth"

**EMET and LOGOS:** "Prevailed" for גָּבַר
**PNEUMA:** "Swelled" for the first verb

**Consensus ruling:** "Prevailed" (EMET, LOGOS). The verb גָּבַר (gavar) means "to be strong / to prevail / to overcome / to be mighty." It is not a verb of physical water-level rising (that would be עָלָה, "to go up") but of powerful domination: the waters are not merely rising but prevailing — mastering, overcoming. PNEUMA's "swelled" is a reasonable English word for rising water but loses the strength-domination sense of גָּבַר. "Swelled" also collapses the distinction between the general rising sense (וַיִּרְבּוּ, "increased / multiplied," the second verb in the verse) and the specific prevailing-strength sense (וַיִּגְבְּרוּ, the first verb). LOGOS correctly notes (Translation Note #11) that גָּבַר carries the sense of water actively overcoming the resistance of terrain. "Prevailed" must be maintained consistently across vv. 18, 19, 20, and 24 to preserve the building rhetorical structure.

---

### Disagreement #19 — 7:19: מְאֹד מְאֹד — double intensifier rendering

**Hebrew:** וְהַמַּיִם גָּבְרוּ מְאֹד מְאֹד — "and the waters prevailed exceedingly exceedingly"

**EMET's position:** "More and more" — captures the progressive intensification but loses the absolute superlative sense of the doubled מְאֹד.

**LOGOS's position:** "Exceedingly greatly" — preserves both intensifiers but produces an English phrase that is slightly awkward.

**PNEUMA's position:** "More and more, greatly" — a compromise; preserves the doubling but separates the two intensifiers with a comma.

**Consensus ruling:** "More and more greatly." This is a minor adjustment to PNEUMA's formulation: removing the comma produces a more fluid English intensifier that is still responsive to the doubled Hebrew. "More and more greatly" reads as a natural English escalation (the waters were already great; now they were more and more greatly so) while preserving both the progressive ("more and more") and absolute superlative ("greatly") senses of the doubled מְאֹד מְאֹד. "Exceedingly greatly" (LOGOS) is grammatically awkward; "more and more" alone (EMET) understates the absolute superlative.

---

### Disagreement #20 — 7:21: גָּוַע — "expired" vs. "perished"

**Hebrew:** וַיִּגְוַע כָּל-בָּשָׂר — "and all flesh expired/breathed its last"

**EMET and LOGOS:** "Expired" — the physiologically precise term
**PNEUMA:** "Perished" — the more general term

**PNEUMA's note:** "The verb גָּוַע (gava') is specifically the breath leaving a body — to expire, to gasp out, to die the death that comes when the breath stops. It is used repeatedly in the book of Job for the moment of death." PNEUMA acknowledges the word's physiological precision but renders it "perished" in the main text without explanation.

**Consensus ruling:** "Expired." PNEUMA's own note correctly identifies גָּוַע as a physiologically specific word — the act of expiring (the breath leaving). The choice to render it "perished" in the main text despite this analysis is a missed opportunity; the verb's specificity here is directly relevant to verse 22's theme (the breath of the spirit of life in the nostrils — it is precisely the expiration, the leaving of breath, that is described). To use the generic "perished" loses the physiological echo that runs from v. 21 (all flesh expired — breath left) to v. 22 (everything in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life — died) to v. 23 (he wiped out every living thing). "Expired" is not archaic in English and precisely renders the Hebrew.

---

### Disagreement #21 — 7:21–23: Formatting of the death catalog

**The question:** Should the extinction catalog of vv. 21–23 be rendered in prose or in a verse/broken-prose format?

**EMET's position:** Continuous prose with verse numbers; the death catalog is part of the narrative flow.

**LOGOS's position:** Prose with paragraph structure; all within standard verse-numbered paragraphs.

**PNEUMA's position:** Broken verse lines, each category on its own line; "Only Noah" as a standalone line. PNEUMA's argument: "I have formatted verses 21–23 as a kind of broken poetry — not because the Hebrew is strictly poetic, but because the events described demand a different pace than prose. The line breaks are not in the manuscript. They are an interpretive choice: to force the reader to stop at each category, to hold each before going on."

**Consensus ruling:** Modified verse formatting with line breaks within verses. The consensus acknowledges that the Leningrad Codex manuscripts do not indicate poetry at vv. 21–23 (the standard prose cantillation accents are used throughout, as visible in Folio 5r). PNEUMA is correct that "the line breaks are not in the manuscript." They are interpretive choices.

However, the consensus agrees with PNEUMA's diagnosis of the rhetoric: the death catalog employs a formally heightened structure — sustained enumeration, parallel clauses, a final restrictive inversion — that distinguishes it from ordinary narrative prose and deserves visual marking. The consensus adopts a hybrid: verse numbers are maintained (essential for the reference text function); within each verse, the catalogue items are arranged on separate lines; the final "And only Noah remained" is given its own line to mark the structural inversion from total extinction to singular remnant.

PNEUMA's formatting is adopted with adjustments: "Only Noah" is not detached from verse 23 but stands as the final line of verse 23, following the list of the destroyed. This preserves verse integrity while giving the contrast its visual weight.

**Minority position recorded:** EMET's continuous prose rendering is the more conservative and formally correct position relative to the manuscript tradition. Readers who prefer prose pacing without the typographical guidance of line breaks should understand that the Hebrew text does not formally mark these verses as elevated over surrounding narrative.

---

### Disagreement #22 — 7:22: נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים — full compound vs. simplified

**Hebrew:** כֹּל אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים בְּאַפָּיו

This is the most significant philological disagreement in the chapter.

**The compound construct chain:** Three terms in a genitive construction:
- נִשְׁמַת (nishmat) — "breath of" — from נְשָׁמָה (neshamah), the breath of Genesis 2:7 (נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים breathed into Adam's nostrils)
- רוּחַ (ruach) — "spirit / wind / breath" — the animating force of Genesis 1:2
- חַיִּים — "life"
- בְּאַפָּיו — "in his/its nostrils"

**EMET's position:** "The breath of the spirit of life" — renders the full construct chain, with both neshamah and ruach explicitly preserved.

**LOGOS's position:** "The breath of the spirit of life" — same as EMET. LOGOS notes explicitly: "The verse 22 phrase נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים is one of the most theologically dense phrases in the flood narrative. It combines two distinct words for breath/life... The combination of both words in a single construct chain is unique in the Hebrew Bible." LOGOS notes that the ESV and KJV both render v. 22 as "the breath of life" (following the LXX's simplification) and that the NASB's "the breath of the spirit of life" is the rendering that preserves the full chain.

**PNEUMA's position:** "The breath of life in its nostrils" — PNEUMA collapses the compound to the simpler nishmat-chayyim formula of Genesis 2:7, deliberately echoing the creation account's specific wording. PNEUMA's note: "What God breathed in at the beginning, the flood has breathed out. The breath that animated the world has been extinguished." PNEUMA is reaching for the Genesis 2:7 echo and chooses to simplify to emphasize that connection.

**Ancient witnesses:**
- LXX (Vaticanus): πνοὴν ζωῆς ("breath of life") — follows the simpler two-term formula, losing רוּחַ
- Targum Onkelos: "breath of the spirit of life" — preserves the compound
- Peshitta: "breath of the spirit of life" — preserves the compound

**Consensus ruling:** "The breath of the spirit of life" (EMET and LOGOS). This is the most consequential vocabulary ruling of the chapter.

The justification for the full compound over PNEUMA's simplification:

1. The Hebrew text at v. 22 is unambiguous: נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים is a three-term construct chain that explicitly includes רוּחַ (ruach). It is distinct from the two-term formula of Genesis 2:7 (נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים). To translate v. 22 as simply "the breath of life" is to render a different Hebrew phrase than what stands in the MT.

2. The compound נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים is unique in the Hebrew Bible. Its uniqueness is not accidental — it is the most elaborate, most theologically loaded breath-formula in the flood narrative, positioned at the climax of the death catalog. The expanded formula signals: this is not merely physical death but the extinction of everything that bore the full animating principle of Genesis 1:2 (the ruach/spirit) and Genesis 2:7 (the neshamah/breath). Both creative acts of God are undone at once.

3. PNEUMA's argument (the Echo of Genesis 2:7 is the primary connection) is textually incorrect: the verse does not simply repeat the Genesis 2:7 formula — it expands it by adding ruach. The expansion is intentional and theologically significant. PNEUMA's note itself says this correctly ("What God breathed in at the beginning, the flood has breathed out") but the translation does not render it.

4. The LXX's simplification is not a guide for the consensus translation; it is precisely the kind of leveling that the consensus is designed to resist when the MT's compound form is theologically significant.

**Minority position recorded:** PNEUMA's "the breath of life" is a defensible simplification with LXX support and succeeds in creating a direct Genesis 2:7 echo that is immediately legible to most readers. The cost — the loss of the explicit ruach term — is documented here.

---

### Disagreement #23 — 7:23: Subject of וַיִּמַח — explicit vs. ambiguous

**Hebrew:** וַיִּמַח אֶת-כָּל-הַיְקוּם — "and [he/it] wiped out every living thing"

**The question:** The verb is third-person masculine singular. Who or what is the subject? No explicit subject is stated.

**EMET's position:** "And he wiped away" — uses the masculine pronoun but acknowledges the ambiguity in the notes: "The subject is ambiguous — the Hebrew verb is third-person singular masculine but the subject is not explicitly stated. God? The waters? The ambiguity may be deliberate."

**LOGOS's position:** "And every living thing was blotted out" — renders as a passive, sidestepping the subject question entirely. The passive construction is grammatically possible but requires converting an active Hebrew verb into a passive English construction.

**PNEUMA's position:** "He wiped out every living thing" — takes the divine agent as the subject without hedging.

**Consensus ruling:** "He wiped out" (PNEUMA). The ambiguity EMET notes is genuine, but in context the most natural reading of the third-person masculine singular verb is God as agent — consistent with the divine agency stated throughout the chapter (v. 4: "I will wipe out"; the LORD who shut the door in v. 16; the flood that the LORD has brought). The waters are an impersonal agent and grammatically unlikely as the stated subject of a transitive Qal verb with an explicit object (אֶת-כָּל-הַיְקוּם). LOGOS's passive conversion is technically possible but departs from the active Hebrew construction in a way that EMET and PNEUMA both resist.

The ambiguity is documented in the notes (see Textual Note on 7:23 in the consensus file). In the main text, the active "He wiped out" preserves the Hebrew's active construction while allowing the reader (with the note) to hold the theological ambiguity between divine act and natural catastrophe.

---

## Summary of Consensus Decisions by Category

### Formatting decisions
- **7:11** — Cosmological clauses set as verse lines after the date formula (modified from PNEUMA's proposal)
- **7:16b** — "Then the LORD shut him in" as standalone sentence with preceding line break
- **7:21–23** — Death catalog presented with line breaks within verses, categories on separate lines

### Major vocabulary rulings
- **שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה (7:2) → "seven pairs"** (not "seven and seven"; not EMET's literal distributive)
- **נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים (7:22) → "the breath of the spirit of life"** (full compound; not PNEUMA's simplified "the breath of life")
- **אֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם (7:11) → "windows of the heavens"** (not "floodgates"; following MT's architectural image)
- **גָּוַע (7:21) → "expired"** (not "perished"; physiologically precise term retained)
- **מָחָה (7:4, 7:23) → "wipe out"** (consistent with GEN_006 ruling)

### Consistent with GEN_006 rulings
- **שָּׁמַיִם → "heavens"** (not "sky") — confirmed at 7:3, 7:23
- **Verse markers maintained** (not merged into prose blocks)
- **יְהוָה → "the LORD"**, **אֱלֹהִים → "God"** (no harmonization of divine names)
- **"Wipe out" for מָחָה** — consistent across 6:7, 7:4, 7:23

### Minor vocabulary and grammar
- **בֹּא → "Come"** (not "Go")
- **אִישׁ וְאִשְׁתּוֹ → "a male and his female"** (relational idiom preserved over neuter)
- **גָּבַר → "prevailed"** consistently (vv. 18, 19, 20, 24)
- **יְקוּם → "living thing"** (not "standing-thing")
- **גָּבְרוּ מְאֹד מְאֹד → "prevailed more and more greatly"**
- **יֶפֶת → "Japheth"** (not "Yaphet" — established English form)

---

## Unresolved Questions for Future Review

1. **שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה (7:2):** The distributive rendering "seven pairs" resolves the Hebrew in a way that is interpretively correct but not formally transparent. If readers require the surface form of the Hebrew to be more visible, a translator's note ("literally: 'seven, seven'") could be added to future editions without changing the main text.

2. **אֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם (7:11):** The "windows vs. floodgates" question remains genuinely open, with the MT and most ancient Semitic versions supporting "windows" and the LXX/Vulgate tradition supporting "floodgates." The consensus adopts "windows" on the basis of the MT and the cosmological architecture of Genesis 1. If comparative ancient Near Eastern evidence for the specific cosmological structure of the firmament's "openings" becomes more precise, this rendering should be revisited.

3. **Formatting of 7:11 and 7:21–23:** The consensus's adoption of modified verse/line-break formatting for these verses is an interpretive choice without manuscript support. It should be reviewed with each edition: is the visual differentiation helping readers engage with the text's own literary structure, or is it imposing a modern poetic apparatus onto ancient narrative prose?

4. **Ambiguity of וַיִּמַח (7:23) subject:** The ambiguity between God and the waters as the agent of wiping is preserved in the notes but resolved to "He" in the main text. A future edition might consider whether the ambiguity itself should be visible in the main text through some typographical or syntactic means.

5. **Divine name pattern and source criticism:** The YHWH/Elohim alternation in chapter 7 — one of the most studied in the Pentateuch — is preserved exactly by the consensus translation as a feature of the received text. The consensus neither adjudicates the Documentary Hypothesis nor erases its textual evidence. This position should be maintained throughout Genesis 6–9 for consistency.

---

*Filed by SOPHIA (Σοφία — Wisdom), consensus arbiter*
*Genesis Chapter 7 arbitration — 24 verses, 23 disagreements recorded*
*Five major disagreements: #2 (seven pairs), #8 (7:11 formatting), #10 (windows/floodgates), #21 (death catalog formatting), #22 (compound breath formula)*
